title: 2025/09/08 - GA (A) (2 of 3)
description: 8 September 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A (1.00pm - 2.00pm) Online Casino Gambling Bill Sport Manawatu Bike Manawatu Inc Tim Lloyd - Chairman Janina Gapper - Secretary Rauktaui Music Therapy Trust Sarah Paterson-Hamlin - Funding Coordinator Wellington North Badminton Association Incorporated Dave White - President and Chair Person Richard Kirby - General Manager Dunedin Choral Society Inc NZ Mountain Safety Council Mike Daisley - Chief Executive Officer
descriptionSummary: (1.00pm - 2.00pm). Online Casino Gambling Bill. Sport Manawatu

chunks.text: Start with Kelly. No problem. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you very much for your submission and for being here in person. Uh, we've got 10 minutes to hear from you, and so please feel free to use as much of that time or leave some time for questions. Cool. Over to you. Thank you, Kiara. Um, Kar. So my name is Kelly Shanks and I am the Chief Executive Officer of Sport AWA two.

Thanks very much for having me here today. Um, I'm re representing Sport Manawa two, um, along with some of our regional sporting organizations and clubs across Ton North UA, Tara and Rural Manawa two. Obviously you can take my submission as rad, but I would like to acknowledge the previous submissions today, including Martin Snedden Royal Ballet nz, SGHC, supergroup and PGF and PGF, who clearly articulated harm.

And I refer any references or questions on harm, uh, to their submission as they articulated this way better than I ever could. I would like to note that the evidence provided by Martin Snedden to the select committee, which supports the information I'm about to talk to. So what he supplied shows that there's ample incredible evidence and research to confirm that the risk of a regulated online casa uh, casino gambling market in New Zealand cannibalizes land-based class four revenues, and therefore funding to communities is real and serious.

Right now, class four grants are the backbone of sport and recreation and r Rohe. Of course, we definitely welcome better models, but none exist today that can replace the $5 million in r Rohe alone in the last year until there's a genuine alternative. Removing this funding without a replacement just means that Tamari and Rangi Ranta children and youth are missing out.

So locally across our region. What does that mean? Well, this funding pays for uniforms in Foxton. It covers whole hire in Danburg and buys equipment for kids teams and fielding this funding also supports kids learning to swim. It keeps our sports facilities maintained and our coaches supported without these funds.

The reality is simple Junior rugby clubs in Lavin would face doubling their fees. Netball competitions in rural tararua, we'd struggle to go ahead and 132 tamari at school would've missed out on the bike track. That now gives them a safe place to ride a bike. So this is not an optional extra, it's what makes sport possible for thousands of children and families across our Ohio.

The proposed online casino gambling bill up to 15 offshore casino operators, but unlike Class four, it doesn't require them to return a single dollar to the community. That means offshore operators profit. Our regions lose out and clubs in places like er, Shannon and ATU where there are very few funding options are left.

Stranded Minister Brooke Van Felden has acknowledged this gap, but argues that requiring community funding could create a perverse incentive with respect. That position leaves our towns and our cities worse off than they are today. This undermines the very purpose of the gambling Act 2003, which is very clear.

It was designed to ensure that money from gambling benefits the community and to give communities a voice in decisions about gambling. This bill breaks with nearly 50 years of principle in New Zealand's law and the scale of what at risk is undeniable. In 2024, more than 8 million in class four funding was distributed across our region alone.

With 66% of that going directed to sport, that's over $5 million potentially gone if this bill goes ahead without any change, and the sinking lid policy continues. So sports are already feeling the pressures. These are quotes from some of our regional sporting organizations that we met with last week.

I've got five quotes. We teach 5,000 children to swim each year, but without funding, we could only reach half of them. In a country that is surrounded by water, that's a risk we simply can't take. Our organization has seen cost jump up by 9,000 this year alone without gaming grants that shortfall gets passed directly onto families and they just can't afford it.

If funding keeps shrinking, kids in rural tarot will have fewer opportunities to play. When sport is taken away, the risk of crime and poor health outcomes they rise. Sport is more than just games. It keeps young people safe, it keeps 'em connected and active. Without the support, our communities will pay the price.

And lastly, we have seen a 30% decline in our funding. We might have to shut our doors if offshore casinos take market share but then return nothing the kids miss out. As families can't afford the rising costs that we'll follow, the impact on facilities will be a mass.

And I do ask you who will maintain these facilities going forward? The long-term result, only those who can afford to play sport will get to play sport. This will have a cascading effect around health and wellbeing for the country. We are not opposing regulation, in fact, we support it, but regulation must protect the very communities who were shoulder the social cost.

We urge the committee to embed the community benefit principle bill into this bill's purpose consistent with the gambling Act 2003, we ask that you ring fence a percentage of online go and casino profits for community distribution and of course keep the system accessible.

So a small rugby club or a school in Shannon as same shot of funding as a large organization in Auckland. I'm certainly not an expert in the bill, but I can talk about community sport impact and the negative impact this bill would have with over four seven, sorry. With over 17,010 to 19 year olds taking part in organized sport across our rugby in a seven day period, the love of sport is definitely high.

Without gaming funding, it will be our most vulnerable populations who will miss out as they will not be able to afford to take part in sport. In ape that is Maori Pacifica multicultural community and our rural isolated youth to conclude the bill as written sends gambling profits offshore and strips regions of the support that makes sport possible.

The sport sector certainly does not want to rely on gaming, however, there is no other current model. So let's regulate responsibly and ensure that every dollar spent on gambling continues to strengthen those communities who shoulder the social cost. Thank you. Uh, I just had a question about, um, well, you've kinda a answered one of my questions, which was you said, I think you had a 35% decrease in funding.

Has that been a, a trend that you've noticed over a a period of time? Yeah, so when we spoke to the, the sports organizations last week and they've, they've talked about a 20 to 50%, um, decline over the last number of years. It's, it's quite significant and the funding has to stretch further and further as the population grows and, and sport general growths.

Yeah. Do you think that's because of, obviously there's cost of living pressures, but do you think one of the factors might be that, um, people are moving away from the traditional types of gambling that have been picked up and able to have a community purpose? Is that, do you have any, is there kind of a link there bet at all?

Sorry, I'm not, I'm not sure. Um, definitely could be, but I couldn't definitely see it. No worries. And just the other thing around the perverse incentive, I think a number of people brought this up in their submissions, um, just for me, that I agree with you that, that that didn't really seem to make that much sense in terms of if people want to support community supporting organizations, there's no barrier to them doing that.

Um, but didn't appear to me, and I'd be interested to know if you were aware of any evidence to say that people were getting into gambling in order to support a community sport organization. Yeah, but they're not getting into gambling to support a community support organization. Good. Thank you. Thank you for clarifying that.

Um, I can't see any hands up from my colleagues there, so, and we have actually almost run out of time. So thank you so much for being here in person. Really appreciate it and for representing, um, Mantu here today in Parliament. Thank you so much. Thank you. All right. Now we've got a colleague of yours from Mantu joining us from Bike Mantu.
I think we had Tim there before he joining us on Zoom.

Tim. Hi. How's things? Good. Thank you. Welcome to the Governance and Administration, select Committee Subcommittee. Thank, we've received for having us. We've received your submission, uh, and we've got 10 minutes to hear from you, uh, today in your oral submission as well. So, uh, we'll let you, um, decide what you wanna do with that time, whether you wanna speak, um, to your submission or, or leave some time for questions. So over to you.

Probably, uh, I mean, Kelly Shanks and Martin Sand have probably nailed most of it, uh, that we do. We deal very closely with Sport Metal too, and also with Cycling New Zealand, uh, with cycling with our youth and also with our veterans as well. And so there has been a massive shortfall in the last, it's been really, really hard in the last, uh, three years, basically post COVID, um, to get funding, um, through, through Pub Charlie and Trillion and, and Lion Foundation.

So one of our big events is the, uh, six hour challenge at Manfield, which gives people an opportunity to, to, uh, ride in a safe environment for six hours effectively. And that's about getting people of all ages on bikes in a semi competi um, nature. Um, a lot of our costs at hiring a venue like Manfield, for example, have gone up by probably 150% in the last three years.

Um, all the traffic management fees have gone up by round about the same. And without traffic management, without first aid, without ProMED, we can't operate an event safely. And if we can't operate events safely, then we just can't operate. That's all there is to it. 'cause that's one of our, um, mentors that we have to stick to. So yeah, I mean, if there was to be no more, um, funding through the gambling agencies would be, it'd be very, very difficult for us to continue as a bike club.

So we're dealing with sport matter too, with, um, with the program that they have going out to schools, to getting kids on bikes. But of course, once you get 'em out on bikes, then teach 'em how to ride safely, then it's another thing to take them to the next level. So, um, goes hand in hand with where we're with support men or two. Thank you. Yeah, we did, um, uh, I think we've had a number of comments from some of the sporting organizations around the community funding, which I think you covered similar to, uh, a similar view

to Sportman or to, I just had a, um, question in relation to another aspect of the bill, which was in relation to something that some other submitters have brought up, which is advertising. Do you have, um, a view on that at all, or, um, whether that, do you have any concerns around that? I know it's kind of not exactly the main sub uh, substance of your submission, but have you considered how that might impact on some of the young people who might be participating in some of your, uh, programs?

When we, when we get funding for these, uh, events from either line or trillion or, um, you know, pub charity, we certainly do, um, advertise the fact that it has come from those opera, um, from those operations. Um, you know, it goes hand in hand that, you know, we need to show due diligence with them that we do thank them for what, what's going on.

But certainly, you know, when there's no wiggle room, when we go for an event for Manfield, for example, is to, um, you know, the higher, you know, the higher of the track or with the first aid, there's no wiggle room with that. So there's certainly a bottom line and, um, every, every year we just get there. And as long as, you know, as well as that with, with any other profits that we do make, we actually, um, send that off to Nui Hospice and so it goes hand in hand.

It doesn't just all fall short, just come into us, then we, you know, provide other charities, um, which we support from there. Thank you. We've got a question from Andy Foster. Look, thank you very much and, and thank you for what you're, what you're doing. Um, just a question.

I was, well the thing you mentioned, not only is the, the revenue going down, but the costs are going up. And does, do you, are you suggesting that previous to, um, previous to the, I mean you mentioned traffic rules, um, which is a matter of great interest to me. Um, are you suggesting that it was a lot more, uh, le a lot less safe before, uh, you had to make these changes?

Or just that the rules and regulations have changed in such a way that it's much more expensive to run things? Um, unfortunately, it, it does vary in a way of traffic things. Andy, as you probably know from from council to council. Um, some councils very strict with what they, um, desire and want and others aren't. I mean, we're lucky enough to have, you know, the oo regional council, um, backing us there and they certainly help us with everything that we can.

Whereas when we, when we come across the Palm North, not so, uh, when we District council, then Yeah. Yeah. So, um, so when we come across the department or the council, they're not quite as, as, uh, as open to this sort of thing as we want. Uh, you go across the warrack and there's very little traffic management, um, needed.

Anyway, this is a whole other story down. I no, I'll, I'd be, I'd be interested to traverse that more with you, but that's where another hat It will be. Yeah. Yeah. So all of these costs, every, every single time we put a traffic management, um, thing in it's applications, it's, it's $120. I do it for nothing. Um, it's, we are incredibly drained with, with, with volunteer work.

And so if we don't, if the pub charity and, and if all of the other online gambling, um, income stops, you know, we're already right at the edge of people having spare time to come and help us. And that's only gonna get worse. No, look, thank you very much for That. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you, Tim.

Um, unless there are any other questions, um, we'll leave it there. And just thank you very much for the work that you do and, uh, for submitting to our committee today. Thank you very much. Hopefully we get a, uh, a positive outcome for all of New Zealanders. Yeah, that's what we are working as well. Thank you so much. Thank you. Cheers. Thank you. So our next, uh, submitters are the Ro Kata Katoi Music Therapy trust.

We've got, I think Sarah there, Sarah Kiara. Kiara, uh, thank you for your written submission and, uh, for joining us today. We've got 10 minutes set aside, uh, to hear from you. Uh, we have had the opportunity to, uh, read your written submissions and I do have some colleagues, uh, who are on the Zoom as well, though you might not be able to see them.

Um, so over to you, KRA. Thank you so much committee for allowing us to present today and allowing us to do it via Zoom as well. Yeah. Uh, I'm here representing Thero, Kato Music Therapy Trust. We provide music therapy to people with disabilities and mental health conditions, uh, about 900 every week in the Hawkes Bay, Auckland and Northern Regions.

We employ 22 people. And you'll be hearing, in fact, I've heard that you've heard from many charities, I'm sure, who rely to a greater or lesser extent on the proceeds of things like lottery commission and pokies trusts for us at ok. This is about a quarter of our fundraising income, and what that represents is over 2000 people with disabilities or mental health conditions who are supported through that funding every year.

And like a lot of people, a lot of groups in the charity sector, we, we do sit uneasily with the reality that such a high proportion of our income is derived from what is essentially a harmful practice. Um, but given the nature of our work in places like children's respite homes, prisons, hospitals, and via referrals from, for example, we are at least confident that Al Mahi supports a lot of those who are negatively impacted from gambling.

You'll also be aware that due to economic conditions, this has been an extremely challenging few months for us in the charity sector. You'll be hearing that a lot today. I am sure, uh, in my decade or more fundraising in our Tarra 2025 has without doubt been the most challenging year in that space. Several of, uh, charities that work in a similar space to us have closed down this year, which has led to an increase in demand that we just can't meet.

And this has led to, you know, when those organizations close down, that leads to redundancies and negative economic impacts. Not to mention, of course, the significant wellbeing impacts on whanau impacted by disability in our space. And I'm sure you're also aware that the charity sector is worth at least 12 billion to New Zealand's, GDP. And so all this leads me to reiterate that this legislation, if it is allowed to progress as it currently is, will enable a significant increase in online gambling options for New Zealanders with no attendant requirement

to return a significant portion of those profits to the community. And that will cause deep lasting irreparable harm to New Zealand. The harm will come in the form of immediate and significant increased risk to problem gamblers and the innocent bystanders in their lives. It'll come in the form of the undercutting of current, currently legitimate gambling activities in New Zealand that returns, um, you'll have the better figure, but at least a hundred million dollars to communities every year.

It'll come in the subsequent reduction of charitable funds available overall in this country. Devastating and already at risk aspect of our economy that is responsible for approximately 5% of the workforce in New Zealand. And virtually the entirety of the volunteering opportunities that New Zealanders rely on, uh, including those attempting to reenter the workforce. And quite a lot of volunteers with us are in that position.

So if this legislation is passed as it is, the results will be devastating and we ask in the strongest possible terms that this bill does not progress beyond sleep committee stage. I'm happy to take any questions. Thank you so much, Sarah. Thanks for the amazing, um, work that you and your colleagues do, um, in relation to music therapy with your, uh, clients around New Zealand.

Um, the question I had is just in relation to, um, the funding sources that you do have for your, uh, trust. So obviously a proportion of them are, are through, um, uh, community grants through gambling. But what, what's kind of the breakup of how you can fund this service? 'cause I imagine it's not a service that many of the, um, end users, um, would be in a position to fund themselves privately.

Absolutely. So private music therapy is available, but what we provide is really heavily subsidized music therapy so that any whanau can access it. So whanau, the actual cost of a session to us is $156, but we are asking whanau for as little as $4 a session for that all the way up to about a hundred dollars a session. And that's, you know, dependent on the circumstance of the whanau themselves.

Um, some people aren't paying anything 'cause we're seeing them in a school or a prison or something like that. And so it's covered elsewhere in terms of how our income breaks down. The client contributions are just under half and the rest is made up of the vast majority of the rest is made up of grants. Um, about a third of which it's a, a quarter of the overall income, and about a third of which is from gambling related things, foundation North, uh, significant supporters, there are most significant supporters followed by lotteries.

And then there's a lot of local government, uh, funds that we tap into typically. And those have been under extreme pressure lately, um, as, as you'll no doubt be aware. And then there's various kind of one-off, uh, public trust administered things. We obviously have donors, but, uh, the number of donors and how much they're able to contribute has gone down, um, as it will have done for, for most in the sector.

Uh, and then we do run events every now and again when we can. Obviously events cost a lot to put on. They don't often yield that much in return more than what you put into them, but we do do them. Um, so yeah, so that's roughly how it breaks down. About 70% of our fundraising is through grants, and about a third of that is through gambling related, um, trust. Thank you. Um, that's, that's really helpful.

I, I just kind of asked that question because I think it's helpful in terms of, um, the committee understanding how much and what the types of activities, the, um, the type, the grants and the, and the money that are received through things like lotteries actually, um, go towards supporting. The last question I had was really about, um, the way that money is distributed.

We've had quite a few submissions today talking about the fact that we should have a mechanism for community funding, but also, um, a function that works in terms of distribution of that funding. And quite a few people have said that, um, having a, uh, having a mechanism that's similar to how it currently works at the moment, but just for online gambling would be sufficient.

What's your experience with that in terms of applying for funds and grants? Do you think there's a better way that, that we could do this? Or is it is, do you, would you agree with them that what we have currently is, is kind of good, but we need more funding behind it? There's absolutely a better way to do it at the moment. There is a huge variety in the amount of time and expertise and knowledge it takes to apply for a grant end to end the application.

And accountability for a grant might take me, uh, who, who's been doing this as a job for nearly a decade, um, and, you know, is fluent English and grew up in this context and all of that kind of thing can take me anywhere from a minimum of two hours all the way up to 30 plus hours to go end to end with a grants process. They are extremely inconsistent in what they require.

Um, the onus on the applicant is extreme, and what that means is that the bigger the charity, uh, the more staff they have, the more likely they are to be successful has been in operation since 2004. I am the first person with this job of applying for grants that has worked here, um, because it hasn't been in position to be able to afford it.

Uh, and a huge amount of charities are obviously still in that position. Um, it's, it really locks out a lot of great initiatives and it is not an even playing field. Um, it needs significant reform. There is definitely better ways that we can be doing this. Thank you so much. Really appreciate that. Um, thank you for your time today and, um, for speaking to the committee and answering our questions.

Yeah, Thanks. Thank you. We'll move now on to our next submitter who we've got joining us, um, by Zoom. And so we've got the, uh, Wellington North Badminton Association Incorporated. And so I understand we've got Dave and Richard, is that correct? That's right. So I'm the chair of our board and Richard is our general manager and he's here to answer the difficult questions.

Fantastic. Well, good to have you both here. We do have a copy of your written submission, but um, we've got 10 minutes to discuss that with you, so over to you. Okay. So just a bit of, I, I'll be fairly short, but, um, we're a regional sports organization in the Wellington North area. Uh, in the 1990s we built and continue to own our own six court badminton hall.

And the hall is used by our two and a half thousand members for various things like club play, events, training, and, um, they also casually hire it from us as well. Um, we are managed by a six person board, a full-time manager, and six part-time staff members or thereabouts, changes from time to time.

Um, the utilize, we've got very high utilization. Um, so the utilization of our hall, uh, between the hours of 8:00 AM and midnight seven days a week is around 80%, which is sort of pretty much at our maximum. Um, our, our revenue is in the order of 600,000 and about 70,000 of this is provided by grant funding.

Um, the funding has contributed to and allows us to carry out significant maintenance items, um, to pay our staff and to support our junior players. Um, over the years we have aimed to try and reduce our reliance on grant funding. Um, we didn't want to be subject to it being cut off from us.

Um, and we are now in a position where degradation of this funding, 'cause we, we have got to the, I guess the last bits that we think we can cope with at our size without having to, um, uh, degrade our, um, services. Um, so degradation would result in a reduction in services and, um, and, and probably reduction in members as well as bought is good for the body and the soul.

Um, as you can tell from these two people in front of you, we provide these services for the good of the community. Um, and as a result of this proposed legislation, we concern that the revenue that we and other like-minded organizations are currently receiving will be directed away from us and lost in the consolidated fund. And that's, uh, that's what I've gotta say.

So yeah, open to you for questions. Thank you. Uh, I, I've looked over your submission and seen, um, the, the financial information in the table that you've put in there in terms of the grants funding. Um, I just wanted to just give you an opportunity to just, uh, let us know the kind of messages that you want us to take from that.

It looks to me like you had a big dip in funding in 2023, um, but have had a kind of significantly increased, um, amount or at least in grants in in 2024. Is it, is it, I understand your point around reliance on grants, but um, just can you explain at all the discrepancy There? Yeah, so it does tend to vary.

It depends on what is happening at the time, um, and, and when funds are allocated. So recently we, um, spent quite a lot of money on our, um, roof and we got funding for that. So we got a a, a good chunk of funding for that. But that then, um, changes the timing, I guess, in terms of whether it happened in one year or another year.

Um, perhaps I'll get Richard to elaborate a little bit more on that. Yeah, so in 20 20, 24, we did have a, we did have some, uh, grant funding from towards the roof. Um, not typically a funder we apply to only for, um, sort of bigger, bigger capital items. So, uh, yes, we did get a, um, a decent grant from lotteries that year, but it was allocated to a project that isn't sort of our day to day activity.

Um, most of our funding we receive, um, you know, does go towards staff wages, events and sports equipment, um, primarily, and yes, it does vary from time to time depending on, um, cutoffs for funders and also the, uh, timeframes of when they've last, uh, given you a successful grant.

Um, sometimes there's some grant funders that will only give you one a year. Um, and then there's some that might be three or four times a year, and it just really depends on the cycle, uh, the cycle of that as well. Um, yeah, so I mean, we are, we are expecting 2025, um, you know, we are resigned to the fact that our funding will be down on 2020 fourth.

Um, but we still are really reliant on it to run our programs and our events, um, and run our association. So you think you'd definitely benefit from a, uh, if there was more money available for grants that would assist you with your operations? Absolutely, yes. Yeah. Yeah. Okay.

Thank you so much for joining us today, and we appreciate your, um, written submission and the information that you, that you gave us. So thank you very much for your time. Okay, thank you. Thanks. Take care. Okay. You too. Bye-bye. Yes, Mike? Yep. Yeah. Are you, would you like to do your submission now? Yes. I'm gonna throw In my out No, you, you won't be, be helpful for us just to make sure that we utilize our time if that's like a chair or, yeah, just if you sit just in that middle one there. So, um, I appreciate, um, you being there. Um, thank you so much for your time and for joining us in person. Do have a copy of your written submission and we do have 10 minutes to hear from you today. So over to you if you'd like to make some comments. And then we'll see if there's any questions from the committee. Good. Thank you. Thank you. Um, so as you would've picked up from the submission, uh, MEC from the first principles level actually supports the, the implementation of the bill, um, in terms of what it's seeking to achieve,

of regulating something which is currently unregulated and with, you know, trying to keep as best possible as legislation can, uh, with changes in patterns of, of people's behavior. So people want to, uh, use gambling as an activity and, um, and enjoy that activity. Um, and, but are currently starting to shift into online modes and, and, and changes in patterns there.

So it's a good thing for legislation to try and keep pace with that. Um, but the core of our submission in terms of, uh, the potential unintended consequences of not returning some of those profits back from, from that gabo gambling industry back into communities as it currently does across, as you would know, across the other, other gambling acts, um, will have some unintended consequences.

And, and for us, uh, just trying to put that into some context, um, at Mountain Safety Council every year, our core funding, we do get some funding, uh, for particular pieces of work, uh, that we implement. Uh, but our core funding has, has traditionally come from the Valerie Grants board for the outdoor safety, uh, committee.

Um, and that's been a, quite a, a long term, uh, in investment into the organization. So that's typically in the scale of one and a half million a year. It varies, but, um, and it hasn't really grown. But, and, and one of the, we'll come back to it, but one of the key reasons of why it hasn't grown is there's been no, no growth of investment into lotteries.

Um, but just to give you a sense of, of, of what that potential unintended conduct, for instance could be, is unlike most of the organizations that we intersect with around the world, uh, that, that do similar things to the Mount Safety Council, um, are funded directly from government. Um, and, and New Zealand is, is not an absolute exception, um, but that the system of funding to our core operations that we've enjoyed for, for decades has been via lotteries.

Um, and so changes within that system can, uh, you know, and have an unintended impact on that. And it give you a sense of scale of, of what we have achieved, particularly in the last decade in terms of your a real strong public, uh, interface and a, in a, in a, in a demonstratable, uh, change in patterns of behavior that have improved safety in the outdoors for the general public that are recreating.

Um, we've got a whole suite of digital tools that people, um, that utilize to, to, to inform themselves and better inform themselves, have a great time in the outdoors, but do it more safely. Uh, tools like Plan My Walk, uh, 750,000, uh, plus, uh, annual users of, of that digital tool in terms of helping people plan, uh, their trips, look at the trips and make sure that their, their activities aligned to their skills.

And it sounds really simple, but it enables people to make, uh, their, their trips more enjoyable, but safer. And all of these things through our evidence base can be attributed to a reduction in search and rescue. So we can empirically demonstrate where we've put these interventions in place. There's been significantly less search and rescue, um, and a general pattern of behavior change, positive behavior change in terms of safer behavior.

So reduction in funding into that proven system, uh, would we believe empirically turn that around, um, and you would an increase in cost to the search and rescue system and through a CC in terms of wider injury systems. So the, the core of it here is it's potentially an unintended consequence.

Consequence, um, and just our submission was, was two parts. One, support for the general principle. Um, but this, this principle of returning some of that profit back to communities has worked for New Zealand very well. It's worked for, if you've heard a bunch of organizations, I'm sure from different parts of the sports sector and recreation and art sector.
